bibliographic.citation: Hermann Varnhagen, ‘XIII. Zu dem “Streitgedichte zwischen Drossel und Nachtigall”,’ Anglia: Zeitschrift für englische Philologie 4 (1881): 207–210.
bibliographic.title: XIII. Zu dem ‘Streitgedichte zwischen Drossel und Nachtigall’

entities.description: The complete article represented by CME00092: a German preface followed by a direct transcription of the incomplete Auchinleck fragment and two textual notes.
entities.label: Varnhagen 1881 article and Auchinleck-fragment edition
notes: Printed p. 211 begins an unrelated article on Hamlet, confirming that pp. 207–210 are the complete item.
Varnhagen signs the note at Greifswald; this is an author dateline, not evidence that the medieval poem was composed there.

rights.basis: The article was first published in 1881, before 1930.
rights.component: 1881 article, transcription, notes, and typography

entities.description: The anonymous Middle English bird debate as an abstract work, distinct from both surviving witnesses and all modern editions. DIMEV describes twelve-line stanzas with the rhyme pattern aabccbddbeeb.
entities.label: The Thrush and the Nightingale
notes: The Digby witness is complete at 192 lines; the Auchinleck witness preserves only 74 lines and begins and ends imperfectly.

rights.basis: The work and both surviving manuscript witnesses are medieval and centuries outside copyright term; this does not grant rights in modern photographs, transcriptions, encodings, or editions.
rights.component: Underlying medieval Middle English literary work and witness text

access.accessMethod: Use the NLS Auchinleck project and current manuscripts-catalog route.
access.entityLabel: National Library of Scotland, Advocates MS 19.2.1 (Auchinleck Manuscript)
access.format: Official manuscript project and catalog record
notes: The manuscripts.nls.uk route presented an access challenge in this environment; the official project site remained directly accessible.

entities.description: The direct manuscript source of Varnhagen's encoded fragment. The Thrush and the Nightingale occupies both columns of f. 279v and is incomplete at both beginning and end.

holding.notes: The principal codex is at NLS; ten leaves detached from the manuscript are now held in Edinburgh, St Andrews, and London collections and are represented separately within the NLS facsimile project.

entities.label: National Library of Scotland, Advocates MS 19.2.1 (Auchinleck Manuscript)
notes: The manuscript was produced in London. That witness-production place is not a secure place of composition for the poem.
The current NLS catalog route was access-challenged in this environment; the official Auchinleck project pages supplied the directly verified physical and textual evidence.

physicalDescription.extent: 331 folios plus 14 stubs in the principal codex; ten further detached folios identified by the project
notes: The project describes 47 surviving quires organized into twelve fascicles.
The present binding is later; losses include leaves and miniatures.

entities.description: A multilingual Worcestershire miscellany containing the complete 192-line witness of The Thrush and the Nightingale at ff. 136vb–138rb.
entities.label: Bodleian Library, MS Digby 86
notes: The rubric at the opening on f. 136vb reads ‘Ci comence le cuntent par entre le Mauuis et la russinole.’
This is a parallel witness and the source of the complete nineteenth-century editions discussed by Varnhagen, not the manuscript he says he transcribed for CME00092.

physicalDescription.format: Religious, medical, and secular verse and prose miscellany
notes: Languages represented in the catalog metadata include Latin, Middle English, and Old French.

reviewNotes: The lineage separates the abstract poem, its two witnesses, nineteenth-century editions and cataloging, modern digital encodings, and image surrogates.

The Auchinleck cache is complete for the 694 image surfaces published by the provider's facsimile project. It aggregates the main NLS codex and detached leaves held at three other institutions and does not include separate covers, boards, spine, pastedowns, or binding views; it must not be described as one complete current physical codex.

Public web access and private research caching do not establish permission to redistribute source files. NLS project terms expressly prohibit unauthorized commercial use or publication; Bodleian images use CC BY-NC 4.0.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a local copy/version of University of Michigan CME item CME00092, a keyed encoding of Hermann Varnhagen's complete four-page 1881 Anglia note. Varnhagen directly transcribed the incomplete 74-line Auchinleck fragment of The Thrush and the Nightingale from NLS Adv. MS 19.2.1, f. 279va–vb. The earlier, complete 192-line version in Bodleian MS Digby 86 is a parallel witness discussed through Wright and Halliwell, Stengel, and Hazlitt, not Varnhagen's direct source for the encoded fragment.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous medieval poet
catalogSummary.date: Medieval poem attested by 1272–1282; scholarly note published 1881
catalogSummary.region: Medieval England; no more precise place of composition is securely established
catalogSummary.title: Zu dem ‘Streitgedichte zwischen Drossel und Nachtigall’

catalogingSubject.label: Zu dem ‘Streitgedichte zwischen Drossel und Nachtigall’
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The complete four-page scholarly note published by Hermann Varnhagen in 1881 and represented by CME00092: a German prose preface followed by Varnhagen's transcription of the incomplete 74-line Auchinleck fragment of The Thrush and the Nightingale. It is not the complete 192-line poem preserved in Bodleian MS Digby 86, the abstract medieval work, either manuscript witness, or the U-M encoding itself.

contentParts.label: German editorial preface
contentParts.summary: Varnhagen identifies prior Digby 86 editions and collation, identifies Laing's earlier Auchinleck printing, and says he is republishing the fragment directly from the manuscript.

contentParts.label: The Thrush and the Nightingale: Auchinleck fragment
notes: The seven XML LG wrappers are editorial grouping/display units; they should not be interpreted as seven complete twelve-line stanzas.
The rhyme pattern aabccbddbeeb and twelve-line stanza form describe the abstract work in DIMEV; the surviving Auchinleck fragment is incomplete at both boundaries.
contentParts.summary: An incomplete 74-line Middle English witness of the bird debate, beginning and ending in lacunae and occupying both columns of folio 279v.

assertion.notes: The manuscript date supplies a terminus ante quem; it is not a precise composition date.

dateStatements.display: Medieval; composed no later than the 1272–1282 copying of Digby 86

scope.description: Abstract medieval poem, not Varnhagen's article
The abstract poem's earliest known surviving witness

extent.description: One complete four-page scholarly note (Anglia 4, pp. 207–210): a short German preface and an incomplete 74-line Middle English fragment. The XML has 81 L elements in seven LG wrappers because five speaker headings and two dotted lacuna placeholders are also encoded as L.

formStatements.description: German prose scholarship followed by a Middle English verse fragment.
The abstract work is in twelve-line stanzas with rhyme pattern aabccbddbeeb; the Auchinleck witness begins and ends imperfectly.

notes: The cataloging record deliberately describes the complete 1881 scholarly note while separately exposing the medieval work and witness layers.
The complete Digby witness is a parallel witness of the abstract poem and a comparison source discussed by Varnhagen; it is not Varnhagen's direct transcription source for the CME fragment.
XML L and LG counts are representation metrics. They must not be promoted to verse-line or stanza counts without auditing headings and lacuna placeholders.

next_steps: Consult modern critical scholarship on the poem and linguistic analyses of both witnesses.
Keep witness production places separate from any future composition claim.
openQuestions.question: Can the abstract poem's composition date and place be localized more narrowly than before the 1272–1282 Digby witness and medieval England?

next_steps: Compare each italic expansion against the cached 1600-pixel f. 279v image and, if needed, request or retrieve a higher-resolution NLS image under applicable terms.
Compare the current NLS diplomatic transcription's supplied and superscript readings without assuming it reproduces Varnhagen's editorial policy.
openQuestions.question: Which italic expansions in Varnhagen and CME correspond exactly to abbreviation marks visible in f. 279v?

scope.description: Production of the earlier complete Digby witness

assertion.notes: Varnhagen explicitly says that he republishes the fragment 'nach der hs.' (from the manuscript).

summaries.value: Varnhagen briefly reviews nineteenth-century prints of The Thrush and the Nightingale and then transcribes from the Auchinleck manuscript an incomplete 74-line version in which the nightingale defends women and the thrush condemns them.

The five speaker headings and two final dotted lacuna rows encoded as L elements are display structure, not seven additional medieval verse lines. The source witness and NLS transcription contain 74 verse lines; DIMEV likewise reports 74 lines and says the fragment begins and ends imperfectly.

notes: This is Varnhagen's article heading and therefore the preferred title for the cataloging subject.
titles.value: Zu dem ‘Streitgedichte zwischen Drossel und Nachtigall’

assertion.notes: Project-supplied discovery translation, not a published alternate title.

titles.value: On the ‘Debate Poem between the Thrush and the Nightingale’

notes: DIMEV's title for the abstract medieval work represented by both the Digby and Auchinleck witnesses.

assertion.notes: The beginning is physically incomplete; the supplied reading 'Lenten is comen ... to toun' belongs to modern reconstruction and is not silently used as the encoded incipit.
The witness breaks off after this line; CME then prints two dotted lacuna placeholders.

work.author: Anonymous medieval poet
work.dateDisplay: Medieval poem attested by 1272–1282; scholarly note published 1881
